morphology.summary: A French-derived noun historically connected to villa or estate-related social status.

etymology.summary: The word traces through Old French to Latin villa-related forms before acquiring moralized negative senses.

semantic_drift.summary: The meaning pejorates from rural or low-status social identity to morally wicked person and then narrative antagonist.

developments.description: Class status becomes a social label.
The moral sense becomes a character role.

present_usage.summary: Modern villain primarily means a wicked person or narrative antagonist.

senses.definition: A morally bad, criminal, or harmful person.

examples.text: The article made the executive the villain of the crisis.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

senses.definition: A character who opposes the hero or central protagonist.

examples.text: The film gave the villain a motive without excusing the cruelty.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

cultural_frame.summary: The word carries class history, moral blame, and story-structure frames.

frames.label: class pejoration
frames.description: Shows how status terms can become moral insults.

frames.label: narrative antagonist
frames.description: Frames a person by story function rather than literal criminality.

pragmatics.summary: Villain can condemn, dramatize, simplify blame, or mark a story role.

stances.label: moral condemnation
stances.description: Marks someone as blameworthy or malicious.

stances.label: dramatic framing
stances.description: Simplifies conflict by casting someone as antagonist.

expansion.paragraph: Villain demonstrates pejoration. A word tied to estate and low-status social identity became a moral label for wickedness and then a narrative role for an antagonist. Modern use may condemn actual harm or simply cast someone as the bad figure in a story.

trace.contribution: The word traces through Old French to Latin villa-related forms before acquiring moralized negative senses.
The meaning pejorates from rural or low-status social identity to morally wicked person and then narrative antagonist.
Villain can condemn, dramatize, simplify blame, or mark a story role.

capsule_summary: A class-marked word that pejorated into wicked person and broadened into narrative antagonist.

unresolved_questions: Add medieval social-history citations for the villein/villain pathway.
